---
title: "Red Tasselflower (Emilia fosbergii)"
slug: "emilia-fosbergii"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622325912/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Red Tasselflower"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Asterales"
tax_family: "Asteraceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Asterales", "Asteraceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622325912/medium.jpg" alt="Red Tasselflower" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/jcarcache">
jcarcache</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/341825583">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Asterales
-   **Family:** Asteraceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=129103)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not available
-   **Rarity note:** Common across Florida.

## Natural history notes

Small, weedy wildflower characterized by its slender stems and clusters of reddish-orange, tassel-like flower heads. In Florida, it is commonly found in disturbed areas such as roadsides, lawns, and fields. It can bloom year-round in warm conditions and is frequently visited by butterflies and other pollinators.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/129103-Emilia-fosbergii)
-   [Local profile](https://mrec.ifas.ufl.edu/resources/weedsbyflowercolor/red/emiliafosbergii/)
